scopecontent.head: Content Description
scopecontent.p: Approximately 400 photographic prints on the topics of climate change, biodiversity, endangered wildlife, and vanishing glaciers.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: James Balog was born in Pennsylvania in 1952. He is an author, nature photographer, mountaineer, and an A.D. White Professor-At-Large. He has worked with many publications including National Geographic.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: James Balog photographs, #8877. Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections, Cornell University Library.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Notes derived from photographer's original inventory.

physdesc.dimensions: All prints 20 x 24 in. (51 x 61 cm)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Images from James Balog's 1991 – 1993 Anima project captured people posing nude with chimpanzees. The series sought to symbolize humanity's ancient connection to our primate ancestors.

did.unittitle: The Old Man and the Ape

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: In 2007 James Balog launched the Extreme Ice Survey (EIS), which deployed a network of time-lapse cameras at various glaciers around the world and produced unconfutable, visual evidence of climate change. Between March and July 2007, the EIS team mounted 24 cameras on rock walls above and alongside glaciers in Alaska, Greenland, Iceland, and northern Montana, documenting how our warming climate has caused the world's ice masses to retreat and thin. Powered by photovoltaic panels, the automated cameras photographed year-round during every hour, or half hour, of daylight. Eventually, a total of 72 time-lapse cameras were intermittently deployed, including in Antarctica, Greenland, South Georgia, and Austria, and at the foot of Mount Everest in Nepal. Most photographs in this sequence were selected from the 1.5 million frames of the Extreme Ice Survey's digital time-lapse imagery. That data is now preserved in the archive of the National Snow and Ice Data Center in Boulder, Colorado. Other images were the product of efforts to repeat aerial or ground-based camera positions year after year.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: In 2005, James Balog received a photography assignment in Iceland for The New Yorker magazine. This assignment led to a creative breakthrough on how photographic evidence could reveal climate change by capturing receding glaciers. This in turn led to a 2006 National Geographic assignment, "Big Melt" (published as a cover story in June 2007). Balog founded the Extreme Ice Survey (EIS) that year, a wide-ranging, ground-based, photographic study of glaciers. In addition to the time-lapse photography, the EIS also produced 12,000+ single-frame images of glacial landscapes, featured in this series. Locations include the U.S. (Alaska and Montana), Canada (British Columbia), Greenland, Iceland, France, Switzerland, and Bolivia.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: James Balog has focused his photographic career on documenting the ways in which humans have altered atmospheric chemistry, resulting in changes in weather and climate. Balog explored the issue in a variety of ways, including photography of five hurricanes, the flooding from many lesser storms, and through documentation of submarine lifeforms.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Earth scientists use the term "plate tectonics" to describe geologic forces that pull and crack the planetary crust and alter the shapes and activities of continents, oceans, mountains and volcanoes. Intrigued by these forces, Balog completed a master's degree in geomorphology—the science of how nature shapes the landscape—at the University of Colorado's Institute of Arctic and Alpine Research. When he later became an environmental photographer, he sought to bear witness to a wide range of natural tectonics and their consequences.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Driving through western South Dakota in 1982, Balog encountered land punctuated by chain link fence enclosures which looked distinctly out of place on the vast prairie. After learning that each enclosure protected an underground silo with an active intercontinental ballistic missile (ICBM), he photographed the environment between 1982 and 1985. Many hundreds of silos dotted the terrain. He was also able to photograph the interior of a silo at Whiteman Air Force Base in Missouri, where Air Force officers practiced launch procedures. Many of these silos were decommissioned after the end of the Cold War.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: From 2010 to 2017, Balog photographed fourteen active wildfires and/or their aftermath. One of the fires, Colorado's Four-Mile Canyon Fire in 2010, came within a mile of burning his own home (the fire torched 6,181 acres and destroyed 162 homes). Balog and his team trained to be wildland firefighters, skills which allowed them to work with a reasonable degree of safety. In 2016, they produced extensive coverage of the Soberanes Fire, south of Carmel, California. Other projects sought to engage with the sculptural, mythic, and transformative character of fire. He created studies of flames outdoors in the Rocky Mountains and at the U.S. Forest Service's Missoula Fire Sciences Laboratory in Montana, using black-and-white infrared cameras as well as conventional color cameras.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Balog created studies of flames at the U.S. Forest Service's Missoula Fire Sciences Laboratory in Montana, using black-and-white infrared cameras as well as conventional color cameras. Others were created outdoors in the Rocky Mountains. The resulting images engage with the sculptural, mythic, and transformative character of fire.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: In 1987, Balog began a series of studio portraits of endangered species. Photographed between 1987 and 1997, all were classified as needing legal protection. As natural habitat destruction threatened the survival of many populations and many animals were separated from the wild nature they once knew, Balog wanted his portraits to symbolize this alienated condition, not the Edenic fantasy of animals in their natural landscapes. His photographs relied on studio styling or settings where nature was fractured by artificial, human constructions, sometimes taking inspiration from commercial fashion photography.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Between 1998 and 2004, Balog and his assistants crossed America to capture 92 specimens of 47 different species of trees. Most of the trees were designated as "champions"—the largest individuals of their species, according to a system of measurement promoted by the Washington, D.C.-based organization American Forests. Most individual trees in this series were rare survivors of the deforestation that followed in the wake of European colonization, agricultural expansion, and industrial settlement. Initially, Balog continued with the stylistic ideas of portraiture he had used in his work on endangered wildlife, winching up huge fabric backdrops behind the trees. As the project progressed, he eliminated the backdrops, moving to multi-frame treatments to capture the enormity of some of his subjects. Along with an athletic team of tree scientists, Balog developed an innovative—albeit hazardous—technique for climbing and rappelling down coast redwood and giant sequoia, photographing them in hundreds of sequential frames. Then, using the individual frames, constructing enormous mosaics.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Schulman Grove, Inyo National Forest, California.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: In the early 1990s, Balog realized that humans, too, along with endangered wild animals, were living in an altered state. By merging biology and behavior with technology, Homo sapiens seemed to be turning itself into a new species. Balog called that species "Techno sapiens," publishing the term in Fortune (July 8, 1996) and Time Digital (April 27, 1998).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: These images appeared at the end of Balog's book, The Human Element (New York: Rizzoli International, 2021), which brought together stories of changing air, ice, and sea level into proximity with the children of the era. One image features his own daughter, Emily, age 11, holding a fragment of a retreating glacier in Iceland. These images acknowledge that children of Balog's time, as well as all children, for all time, will be trapped in the consequences of environmental modification wrought by the societies before them.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: James Balog made his first photographs at age eight or nine, using his mother's Brownie camera, to photograph squirrels and rabbits in Pennsylvania. But the only adults around him who really cared about animals were hunters. With their guidance, he became a hunter too. By the age of 18, he had begun to question the morality of killing animals for recreation. But when he listened to the wail of a wounded rabbit as it died, he abandoned hunting as a form of sport. These images are from one of Balog's earliest "personal projects," distinct from his later professional assignments. They formed the subject of his first book, Wildlife Requiem (New York: International Center of Photography, 1984) and became a way to reconsider his ties to hunting. Decades would pass before he realized how much synchrony exists between hunting and photography. Each involves an intense search for the subject of interest, staring at it with great concentration using an instrument of technology, then pressing a trigger at a penultimate moment. But, he wrote, "If hunting negates life, photography affirms it."
